description: Apollo 15 Flight Journal - Day 4, part 3: The Descent Orbit

tokens: Apollo 15 has just gone behind the Moon near the end of their second orbit.

While out of contact with Earth, the only planned item is the Descent Orbit Insertion (DOI) burn which is quite critical, so just in case they burn the SPS for slightly too long and lose too much velocity, the Flight Plan includes details of the adjustments they should make if the change in velocity is greater than planned.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: Since LOI, their orbit has measured about 294 by 109 km (159 by 59 nautical miles) with the high point, or apocynthion, occurring near the landing site at Hadley Rille.
The DOI burn will occur at this orbit's low point, or pericynthion, on the opposite side of the Moon from Hadley.
The burn, which is intended to slow the spacecraft by 213.9 fps (65.2 m/s), will have the effect of dropping the high point until it becomes the orbit's low point of only about 16 kilometres.
Since an accurate landing depends on the precision of this velocity change, the crew must adjust for any errors manually.

Also, as there is some uncertainty about the shape of the Moon, and considering that some of the mountains adjacent to the landing site rise 3.4 km (1.8 nm) above the plain at Hadley, burning the SPS longer than planned is particularly undesirable.
A small overburn of the 24.5-second SPS firing could risk the spacecraft impacting with the lunar surface.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: If the velocity is 1 to 2.2 feet per second under what is desired, the minus-X thrusters would be used to increase the stack's velocity (remember, the docked CSM-LM is facing backwards).
As these thrusters impinge upon the LM, they cannot be used for larger velocity corrections.
The Flight Plan instructs that if the velocity correction is more than 2.2 feet per second, the entire stack should be turned 180 degrees so that the forward facing engines can be used.
Correcting for a velocity between 2.2 and 10 feet per second low can be accomplished using the plus-X thrusters.
Corrections larger than 10 feet per second are performed using the SPS.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: In case there is a gross error in the burn which is not picked up by the crew, provision is made for a "bail-out burn".
Soon after the DOI burn, the spacecraft is maneuvered into the correct attitude for the bail-out burn should it be needed.
Immediately after AOS, Mission Control will use radio tracking information to independently check the geometry of their new orbit.
If they are not happy with it they will instruct the crew to carry out the bail-out burn, based on details given at83:26 in the Flight Plan.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: After a nominal burn, the new pericynthion will actually be about 250 miles to the east, or uprange of Hadley, and this will be the point from whichFalcon'sdescent will eventually commence in a maneuver called Powered Descent Initiation or PDI.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: Although many terms are used for the high and low points in a lunar orbit, such as "apolune" and "perilune" or "aposelene" and "periselene" respectively, the correct terms in this context are "apocynthion" and "pericynthion" because they refer to an orbit around the Moon of a spacecraft that came from Earth as opposed to one that was launched from the lunar surface.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: Rev 3 begins at about 080:41.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: Flight Plan page 3-089.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: This is Apollo Control at 83 hours, 9 minutes.
We're about a minute, 45 seconds away from the no-burn acquisition time.
We'll stay up through that period, and up to the normal time.
We're about 5 and a half minutes away from the nominal AOS time given a DOI burn.
We do have an announcement.
Astronaut Jack Schmitt who is the backup Lunar Module Pilot for Apollo 15, will hold a news conference at 8 am tomorrow in the MSC main auditorium.
Subject will be the Apollo 15 EVAs on the lunar surface.
transcript.speaker: PAO

tokens: We've passed the no-burn AOS time now, with no signal.
3 minutes, 30 seconds away from AOS on a nominal burn.
transcript.speaker: PAO

tokens: We have AOS on the Command Module.
transcript.speaker: PAO

tokens: 15, this is Houston.
transcript.speaker: Henize

tokens: Hello, Houston, Apollo 15.
The Falcon is on its perch.
transcript.speaker: Scott

tokens: Which is another way of saying that they believe they are in the correct orbit for the LM,Falcon,to fly off and do its stuff tomorrow.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: Good to hear you coming around that corner.
How do things look?
transcript.speaker: Henize

tokens: Okay.
Burn status report.
Burn was on time.
The burn time was about 24.0 - about half a second shorter than predicted.
There was no trim; residuals were plus .6 [fps], plus .0, minus .1; Delta-VC, minus 4.4; fuel 29 - 29.25 [per cent of full]; and the oxidizer was 29.55; unbalance, 100 increase.
[Pause.]
transcript.speaker: Scott

tokens: As it monitored the progress of the burn, the computer decided when there had been enough change in the spacecraft's velocity and shutdown the engine when appropriate which was half a second before the estimated time.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: Scott, from the1971 Technical debrief:"The DOI was again a nominal burn.
We shut down on time manually, but the G&N beat us to it.
I guess, Al, you could see I put my hands on the switches and timed it.
When the time ran out, I put the switches down.
Al could watch the PC[combustion pressure], and I guess he saw it.
[To Worden] Why don't you just say what you saw?"
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: Worden, from the1971 Technical debrief:"Well, I heard Jim counting down.
I knew Dave was ready to throw the switches, and just as his hand started to move, the PCdropped off.
So the automatic shutdown and Dave's shutdown were almost simultaneous; except the automatic shutdown was, I think, just before his.
It was perfect."
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: Scott, from the1971 Technical debrief:"I took a stopwatch along because we were timing it in tenths of seconds, [which] was necessary for DOI.
I think that unless you really have a double failure, you can't get in trouble on DOI, I don't think that is the problem that some people might have thought it was some time ago.
That is a good, solid method of getting into a low orbit.
Very little chance of getting into a bail-out situation."
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: Thank you, Dave.
We copy all that.
[Pause.]
transcript.speaker: Henize

tokens: Hey, that's an interesting astrophysical observation.
transcript.speaker: Henize

tokens: Comm break.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: Tsiolkovsky is a large, 200-km crater on the far Moon's side which was first photographed by the Soviet probe Luna 3 on 7 October 1959.
In the poor imagery of the time, its dark, mare-like interior made it stand out from the other craters that pepper the far-side.
The triumphant Soviets, in the manner of all explorers, promptly and appropriately named it after the Polish-Russian pioneer of spaceflight theory, Konstantin Tsiolkovsky (1857-1935).

A dominant, W-shaped central peak of light coloured highland material rising out of the dark material makes this crater particularly distinctive and striking and it will be one of Al's major subjects for visual observation during his solo scientific program.

As the spacecraft approaches a pericynthion of 18.5 km (10 nautical miles), its altitude is falling and Dave is noticing how the spherical shape of the Moon causes the central peak of Tsiolkovsky to appear to rise above the crater's rim.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: AS15-91-12397 was taken later in the mission using a wider-angle lens and looks south into Tsiolkovsky showing the irregular shape of the dark area.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: And, Houston, 15.
The G&N [Guidance and Navigation computer] had us in a 58.4 by 10.0 [nautical mile, 108.2 by 18.5 km orbit].
transcript.speaker: Scott

tokens: We copy, 15.
transcript.speaker: Henize

tokens: Long comm break.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: After the execution of the burn, the last item detailed in the checklist for the thrusting operations is Verb 16, Noun 44.
This command displays the altitudes, in nautical miles, of the orbit's pericynthion and apocynthion, relative to the height of the landing site.
It also shows the time taken to make one orbit, the orbital period.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: Houston, 15.
We're in an attitude now in which we cannot see the surface.
But our initial impressions after the burn, when we could see the surface, is that we were rolling in on a high-angle pass.
transcript.speaker: Scott

tokens: We copy, 15.
[Pause.]
transcript.speaker: Henize

tokens: A "high-angle pass" comes from a phrase used by tactical fighter pilots.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: Scott, from1999 correspondence:"This generally means beginning a steep-angle dive-bombing run, as it might apply to a feature on the surface."
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: As the father of one of the editors of the Apollo Flight Journal is a retired fighter pilot, we thought we would seek his explanation of the term.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: Lt.
Col.
Frank J.
O'Brien, USAF, Ret.:"A high angle pass is a method of dive bombing that minimizes your exposure to ground fire, and is used for what we called 'slick' bombs, or those with no retarding device.
In other words, for all dumb bombs of the 500#, 2000# variety.
(When we were dropping napalm or 500# high drag bombs, we used about a 15 degree dive angle and started the run from about 3,000 feet.
A lot more exciting experience.) You started by circling the target at about 13-15 thousand feet, depending on terrain elevation and your planned drop altitude.

When cleared by the FAC (the Forward Air Controller; a pilot in a small, slow and low-flying aircraft who identifies targets on the ground), you started a diving 90 degree turn to the attack heading, and tried to establish a 60 degree dive angle.
Shallower dive angles were not used because of more exposure to ground fire.

The first few times you try this steep dive angle it seems a bit hairy, since we trained at George [Air Force Base, in the Mojave desert of California] with 45 degree dive angles, but when they are shooting at you it is a great motivator."
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: Lt.

Col O'Brien added that for a more detailed explanation of dive bombing he would refer us to an "excellent work by a little known local author." The title isThe Hungry Tigers, the topic in question being on pages 106-122.
He did not add that the book was, in fact, written by Lt.
Col Frank J.
O'Brien, USAF, Ret.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: And, Al just added, we were.
transcript.speaker: Scott

tokens: True enough.
True enough.
[Long pause.]
transcript.speaker: Henize

tokens: 15, this is Houston.
You're Stay in the DOI orbit, and we have an orbit for you of 58.8 by 9.5 [nautical miles, 108.9 by 17.6 km].
transcript.speaker: Henize

tokens: Very good, Houston.
58.8 - 9.5 and we're Stay.
Thank you.
[Long pause.]
transcript.speaker: Scott

tokens: On reacquiring the spacecraft around the Moon's eastern limb, the tracking stations on Earth began measuring its velocity and distance from Earth from frequency and delay changes in the received radio signal.
From this, they calculated the orbit achieved by the DOI burn.
They are in good agreement with the values calculated by the spacecraft's computer.
Had Mission Control been unhappy with the orbit, they would have instructed the crew to "bail out".
The crew would then have used the emergency burn given at83:26 in the Flight Planto alter their orbit.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: Woods, from1999 correspondence with Scott:"Since the DOI burn has the same effect as the LM DOI burns of Apollos 11 and 12, do you know why they did not have a bail-out contingency when A15 did?"
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: Scott, from1999 correspondence:"For one thing, with the CSM we could go back up to 60 nautical miles and try DOI again - but with a LM DOI, the LM is committed and the only alternative is an abort.
With the CSM, by going to the bail-out attitude immediately, we would be ready for such a contingency and would lose very little orbital motion and timing - to set up a second LOI."
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: You have a Go to proceed to the landmark observation attitude.
transcript.speaker: Henize

tokens: Roger, Houston.
Thank you.
transcript.speaker: Scott

tokens: And would you please give us Auto on the High Gain [Antenna]?
transcript.speaker: Henize

tokens: Auto.
transcript.speaker: Scott

tokens: Long comm break.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: Apollo 15's attitude is currently fixed with respect to the stars, and as it orbits, the Moon has gradually moved away from its windows.
The "landmark observation attitude" will reorient the spacecraft in preparation for viewing landmark J-1 such that the optics are facing the Moon as they pass over Mare Serenitatis.
For a while they can see the Moon's surface again as the spacecraft changes attitude.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: Okay, Houston, 15.
We've got a little bit of visual - visibility now, and we're down in Crisium.
[Pause.]
transcript.speaker: Scott

tokens: Dave is literal in his use of "down" as they are approaching their pericynthion and are descending as they cross Mare Crisium.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: Roger, 15.
[Long pause.]
transcript.speaker: Henize

tokens: And it looks like we have enough altitude to get up over the [mountains on the] western rim!
transcript.speaker: Scott

tokens: You know, I sure hope so.
[Long pause.]
transcript.speaker: Henize

tokens: But it sure looks like we're looking up at some of those fellows [the mountains] down there.
transcript.speaker: Scott

tokens: That must be sort of exciting, skimming along down there over the waves.
transcript.speaker: Henize

tokens: That's amildword for it.
[Pause.]
transcript.speaker: Scott

tokens: Woods, from1999 correspondence with Scott:"Was it the spacecraft's apparent speed that struck you, or a sense of low-flying rather than orbiting or some other impression that prompted your comm?"
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: Scott, from1999 correspondence:"This comment is due to not so much the speed, as speed is not apparent when still that high over the surface - its the elevation, or angle, of the horizon (mountains) above the 'normal' horizon, whereby the horizon is now higher in the window due to the mountains."
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: Hey, can you see anything more about those sw - swirls?
Anything...
transcript.speaker: Henize

tokens: I think I...
transcript.speaker: Scott

tokens: ...interesting?
transcript.speaker: Henize

tokens: No, we're too close.
And right now I've just finally picked out the rim of Proclus, and we're just about level, altitude-wise, with the - the rim of Proclus.
I cannot see down into it.
I can see just a tad of the southern wall.
I guess we're just north of it.
I can see some large blocks on the outer walls.
transcript.speaker: Scott

tokens: Roger.
transcript.speaker: Henize

tokens: But I'd say we're definitely at an altitude even with the top of the rim of Proclus.
transcript.speaker: Scott

tokens: Long comm break.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: Dave's comment illustrates the low altitude of their flight path at this point, and their altitude is dropping yet as they approach their pericynthion just east of Hadley.
However, they are not really as low as the rim of Proclus.
Rather, the curve of the Moon between them and Proclus means that the crater, sited south of them, will be slightly tilted away from them.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: Okay, Houston, we're coming up on the edge of Serenitatis now and I can look out and see a rille that runs parallel with the eastern edge.
We're taking some pictures.
transcript.speaker: Irwin

tokens: Roger, I suspect that's Littrow Rille.
[Long pause.]
transcript.speaker: Henize

tokens: Although Littrow Rille is beyond the shore of Mare Serenitatis, its arcuate form, roughly parallel to the basin rim, suggests it is related to Serenitatis as a result of extensional tectonics, probably as a result of sinking of the mare basalt.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: And after Littrow Rille, you should be coming up on le Monnier.
Do you have much chance of seeing down at that angle?
[Pause.]
transcript.speaker: Henize

tokens: I doubt it, Karl.
At this attitude for the landmark tracking, the windows are almost out of it.
Jim's got some visibility out window 5.
transcript.speaker: Scott

tokens: During landmark tracking, the crew uses the 28-power sextant to view the lunar surface.

Since the sextant has a limited field of view, the spacecraft must have the optics pointed towards the surface, and, unfortunately for the sightseeing crew, the placement of the optics, on the opposite side of the spacecraft from the windows, makes it more difficult to see out.
This may explain why these two photos are a little foggy, if the camera was viewing obliquely through the window glass.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: Karl, we just passed the wrinkle ridges about a quarter of the way across Serenitatis, and the - there is a - definitely a topographic high in the middle of the wrinkled portions.
[Pause.]
transcript.speaker: Irwin

tokens: Roger, 15, we copy.
[Pause.]
transcript.speaker: Henize

tokens: Are those ridges smooth, or do they show signs of lots of cracks in them?
transcript.speaker: Henize

tokens: I was impressed with the smoothness of the - the raised portion.
transcript.speaker: Irwin

tokens: Roger.
[Long pause.]
transcript.speaker: Henize

tokens: Jim is describing Dorsa Smirnov, a very prominent ridge running about 130 km in a roughly north-south direction across the mare as they view it.
From further away, the ridge is seen to form an arc which is concentric with the rim of the Serenitatis basin.
Henize is prompting him to look for features which will pin down their origin, now known to be the result of compression.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: Mare Serenitatis exhibits some of the best examples of wrinkle ridges and arcuate rilles on the Moon.
These features are related and both are caused by the sinking of the relatively dense mare lavas with respect to the surrounding terrain.
The centre of the mare sinks further than the edges.
In the case of Mare Serenitatis, there is a smooth drop of 200 metres from the shore to the centre.
Wrinkle ridges form where compression of the mare surface takes place and causes uplift.
The very centre of the mare may display a circular formation of wrinkle ridges which can be mistaken for a ghost crater.
Radial ridges may also form.
Sometimes wrinkle ridges can betray the existence of features in the basin, especially ancient crater rings, now overlain by the layers of solidified lava which filled the basin to form the mare.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: Both inside and outside the mare shore, rimae or rilles are often seen running roughly parallel to the basin rim, forming arc-shaped grooves where expansion has occurred.
Often the land between two parallel faults drops slightly to produce a feature known as a 'graben'.
Rimae Littrow, mentioned by Jim before he reached Serenitatis is a good example of this type of rille.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: Okay.
I'm taking several pictures of the wrinkled ridges that run roughly north and south in Serenitatis.
transcript.speaker: Irwin

tokens: Readers should note that many of the smaller craters on the Moon visible from Earth are given the name of a prominent adjacent crater followed by letters of the alphabet.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: Excellent, Jim.
transcript.speaker: Henize

tokens: In the one we just passed over, there were some vertical fractures, definite vertical relief, in the smooth portion of the raised wrinkled ridge.
The fractures were also running north and south.
transcript.speaker: Irwin

tokens: Roger, Jim.
We copy.
[Long pause.]
transcript.speaker: Henize

tokens: Karl, just a spectacular view as we glide across the Sea of Serenity, and I'm taking a picture now of a sinuous rille out to the north.
transcript.speaker: Irwin

tokens: Roger, Jim.
transcript.speaker: Henize

tokens: Comm break.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: Al is attempting to track landmark J-1, a feature on the surface of Mare Serenitatis just east of Dorsum Owen, to check the accuracy of the guidance system's knowledge of the spacecraft's orbit.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: The spacecraft is approaching its 18-km pericynthion.
To make an earthly comparison, the supersonic aircraft, Concorde, cruises at about the same altitude above Earth, but Apollo 15 is travelling three times faster at 5,960 km/h.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: Okay.
We're approaching the mountains now on the western side of Serenitatis.
At this point, there's a wrinkle ridge running to the - the northwest.
[Pause.]
transcript.speaker: Irwin

tokens: This ridge is an exception to the predominant north-south run of ridges in this area of the mare.
It may reflect subsurface structure connected with the hills to which it is joined.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: Karl, this is Al.
transcript.speaker: Worden

tokens: Go ahead Al.
transcript.speaker: Henize

tokens: Okay, Karl.
I just finished the observation on J-1, and everything looked fine from my standpoint.
[I] could track it very smoothly.
And in fact, I took a couple of marks on it, if anyone's interested.
transcript.speaker: Worden

tokens: Very good, and we have them down here.
transcript.speaker: Henize

tokens: Okay.
[Pause.]
transcript.speaker: Worden

tokens: We were sitting down here wondering how you were ever going to find that little bugger.
No trouble, huh?
[Pause.]
transcript.speaker: Henize

tokens: Not that much trouble, Karl.
transcript.speaker: Worden

tokens: Very good.
transcript.speaker: Henize

tokens: Seemed like there was plenty of time waiting on it.
[Long pause.]
transcript.speaker: Worden

tokens: 15, does it look like you are going to clear the mountain range ahead?
transcript.speaker: Henize

tokens: Karl, we've all got our eyes closed.
We're pulling our feet up.
transcript.speaker: Irwin

tokens: Open your eyes.
That's like going to the Grand Canyon and not looking.
transcript.speaker: Henize

tokens: Comm break.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: The mountains of the Apennines reach almost a quarter of the spacecraft's altitude and the sensation of speed is enhanced by their proximity to the surface.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: Bodies in elliptical orbits, like Apollo 15's descent orbit, move faster when they are nearest to the object around which they are orbiting.
However, compared to the size of the Moon, the difference between their pericynthion and apocynthion heights is small with little difference in their absolute velocity between these points.
However, their close proximity to the Moon does affect their apparent speed.
At apocynthion, 110 km up, the terrain parades past at a gentle 0.1° per second.
At pericynthion, only 17.6 km altitude, craters and rilles sweep by at over 5° per second, mountain peaks even faster and, being much lower, the sense of perspective is more dramatic.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: Al is completing a P52 platform realignment, option 3, using the landing site's orientation as the reference point for the platform.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: 15, This is Houston.
I have the camera photo PADs.
transcript.speaker: Henize

tokens: Okay, stand by.
We're just noticing the cabin temp here, and also that the outlet temp is up to about - the glycol about - outlet temp's up to about 70[° Fahrenheit].
[Long pause.]
transcript.speaker: Scott

tokens: We copy, and EECOM says that's normal and they're on their way down now.
transcript.speaker: Henize

tokens: Okay, fine.
Thank you.
transcript.speaker: Scott

tokens: And, Karl, I'm ready to copy the - the Map Camera PAD.
transcript.speaker: Irwin

tokens: Roger.
They're in the Flight Plan at 84:32 and 84:39.
Mapping Camera: T-start, 84:42:23; T-stop 84:54:14.
The Pan Camera times are the same as the Mapping Camera's times and we would like to change a shutter speed at 84:24.
Instead of 125th, we want 1/250th at 84:24.
transcript.speaker: Henize

tokens: Camera PADs will become a regular occurrence during lunar orbit when Al will start and stop the two main cameras in the SIM bay, the Mapping Camera (which is two cameras in one; the Metric Camera and the Stellar Camera) and the Panoramic Camera.

Most photography with these two cameras takes place just to the daylit side of the terminator to take advantage of the low Sun angle and the long shadows which help to make topographic features stand out.
The above times refer to when the spacecraft passes from lunar night to day over the far-side, just as they begin their fourth revolution.

The change in shutter speed refers to photography, using the onboard Hasselblad and very high speed black and white film, which will occur at the same time as the Mapping and Panoramic Camera operation.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: Okay.
Understand 84:24.
We'll change to F 250 [means 1/250] rather than 125; and on the Map PAD, it's 84:42:23 and 84:54:14, and the Pan Camera PAD's the same.
transcript.speaker: Irwin

tokens: That's all correct.
transcript.speaker: Henize

tokens: Long comm break.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: 15, we have your torquing angles.
transcript.speaker: Henize

tokens: Very long comm break.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: Flight Plan page 3-90.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: Houston, 15.
transcript.speaker: Worden

tokens: Go ahead, 15.
transcript.speaker: Henize

tokens: Okay, Karl.
Got a few numbers for you on the [boom] extension times.
We started the whole thing off at 84:06:30, and got the covers open, the Mapping Camera extended, and the extension was about 3 minutes instead of 4.
Then we deployed the Gamma-ray and the Mass Spec.
booms for barber pole plus 2 seconds [to release the tiedowns].
The Gamma-ray turned barber pole after 6 seconds, and Mass Spec.
turned barber pole immediately.
And I was suspicious of the Mass Spec., so we tried it again and got the same results.
Turned the X-ray, On, at 84:15:10 and the Laser Altimeter, On, at 84:15:30.
transcript.speaker: Worden

tokens: The above report is as required in the Flight Plan.
Apollo 15 is the first mission to carry the SIM bay and is essentially a test flight for it.
Everyone involved is therefore very interested to learn how well it performs, especially its various mechanical devices which have never been tested in the freefall vacuum of space.

Throughout, Al will keep a note of the time it takes for the three deployable instruments to extend and retract and Mission Control can watch changes in the current going to the motors to monitor the health of these mechanisms.
Al will also keep a note of when the non-imaging experiments are switched on and off.
Investigators can then relate their data to the timeline and the absolute position of the spacecraft, a data analysis process that will take months to complete.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: Roger, Al.
We copy.
transcript.speaker: Henize

tokens: 15, This is Houston.
Would you verify that the X-ray is On?
[Pause.]
transcript.speaker: Henize

tokens: 15, Roger.
That's verified.
transcript.speaker: Worden

tokens: Thank you.
[Long pause.]
transcript.speaker: Henize

tokens: We have not.
transcript.speaker: Henize

tokens: This may be a miskeying by Karl Henize.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: Comm break.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: 15, this is Houston.
All systems are looking good down here, and until we tell you otherwise, all the AOS's are as in your Flight Plan.
transcript.speaker: Henize

tokens: Roger.
transcript.speaker: Scott

tokens: We're ready to go.
[Laughter.]
transcript.speaker: Irwin

tokens: We both like to hear that.
transcript.speaker: Scott

tokens: Roger.
transcript.speaker: Henize

tokens: Very long comm break.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: This is Apollo Control at 84 hours, 21 minutes.
We've had Loss Of Signal as Apollo 15 turns the corner to go behind the Moon.
We'll acquire on the 4th revolution at 85 hours, 8 minutes, 47 seconds, and as you heard in that last [but one] exchange, the scientific instrument bay, the SIM bay is in business now.
At 84 hours, 21 minutes; this is Mission Control, Houston.
transcript.speaker: PAO

tokens: Apollo 15 has gone behind the Moon and will commence its fourth revolution while over the far side.
As the spacecraft crosses the terminator from night to day, the Panoramic and Mapping Cameras are activated for 11 minutes and 51 seconds to take advantage of the low angle lighting.
The onboard Hasselblad is configured with the 80-mm lens and magazine R, containing very high speed (6,000 ASA) black and white film.

Compare this to the film used aboard the Lunar Orbiter probes of 5 years earlier which was rated at only 2 ASA to give it some tolerance to radiation in the lightweight construction of that pioneering series of spacecraft.
6 images are scheduled to be taken right on the terminator.
These are AS15-98-13303 to 13308.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: The compilers of theApollo 15 Index of 70-mm Photographsguess that the first of these very contrasty images may be of the Montes Haemus, the range of mountains on the rim of Serenitatis, but it seems likely that all six images are of the same, unidentified far-side landscape.AS15-98-13308is one of the clearest of these images which are otherwise difficult to distinguish.
Meanwhile, the crew begin their meal period.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: With the photography completed,the two booms, each more than 7 metres long, are extended from the SIM bay.
One boom carries the Gamma-ray Spectrometer and the other, the Mass Spectrometer.
The booms take the instruments away from possible sources of contamination near the spacecraft and this is their first deployment.
Again, Al times how long it takes for these to extend by monitoring the talkback indicators adjacent to the deploy switches.
He records the results in the Flight Plan.
The booms will be retracted during burns as they are not designed to withstand the forces of engine firings.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: The same ingenious mechanism is employed for both booms.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: Each has two spools of tempered steel tape which, when unrolled, form themselves into a 'c' profile; much like the way a handyman's measuring tape unrolls to form a curved cross-section which stiffens it.
The spools are arranged so that as the two tapes come off and take up their tubular profile, one does so inside the other.
A cable to connect the instrument to the SIM bay is wound around the mechanism's housing and is pulled out around the boom during deployment.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: Flight Plan page 3-91.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: The final task on this far-side pass is to switch the Mass Spectrometer's ion source to Standby.
This brings on heaters to drive out gases within the instrument for the next hour until it is switched on at 086:00:00.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: Rev 4 begins at about 080:42:30.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: This is Apollo Control at 85 hours, 8 minutes.
We're about 20 seconds away from Acquisition Of Signal.
We'll stand by.
transcript.speaker: PAO

tokens: 15, this is Houston.
transcript.speaker: Henize

tokens: Hello, Houston.
Five by [five, meaning 'reading you loud and clear.']
transcript.speaker: Scott

tokens: Roger.
Like to remind you to configure the DSE as per the Flight Plan.
transcript.speaker: Henize

tokens: Roger.
transcript.speaker: Scott

tokens: Comm break.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: Karl is asking the crew to rewind the DSE tape so the ground can play it back to check the spacecraft's performance and collect the SIM bay data gathered during LOS.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: This is Apollo Control.
We're getting good data both from spacecraft systems and from the SIM bay.
transcript.speaker: PAO

tokens: Houston, 15.
transcript.speaker: Scott

tokens: Go ahead, 15.
transcript.speaker: Henize

tokens: Say when we get down to the presleep checklist here in about 50 minutes or so, I wonder if you might give us your best guess on the probability of a DOI trim tomorrow.
transcript.speaker: Scott

tokens: Okay.
We'll work on that information.
transcript.speaker: Henize

tokens: Okay.
Appreciate it.
[Long pause.]
transcript.speaker: Scott

tokens: Dave, we can give you a pretty good guess at that now.
It seems to be unlikely that we're going to need a DOI trim.
I've got a couple of trajectory numbers here if you'd like to copy them.
transcript.speaker: Henize

tokens: Yeah, it'd be interesting.
Go ahead.
transcript.speaker: Scott

tokens: Our tracking data tells us that your current orbit is 58.2 by 9.1 [nautical miles, 107.7 by 16.8 km].
And, tomorrow morning at wakeup time, it'll be 58.6 by 8.7 [nautical miles, 108.5 by 16.1 km].
[Pause.]
transcript.speaker: Henize

tokens: Events in the Moon's past bequeathed it with a gravity field which, to scientists of the late 1960s, seemed unexpectedly uneven.
Regional concentrations of mass intensify the gravity over certain areas.
Consequently, the orbits of small bodies like spacecraft are unstable in the long term.

Known as "mascons," short for "mass concentrations," these mascons are mostly associated with some of the great circular impact basins (Imbrium, Serenitatis, Crisium and Orientale) and are believed to be primarily due to dense mantle material being brought nearer to the surface by the basin-forming impact events, and, to a lesser extent, by the bulk of dense lavas now lying within those basins.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: Since mascons are largely on the near-side, they accelerate spacecraft slightly when compared to the far-side.
Over time, orbits are modified by being lowered on the near-side and raised on the far-side until, if not corrected, they cause the spacecraft to impact the Moon.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: No previous spacecraft have matched Apollo 15's ground track and ground controllers have not accurately characterised the effect of mascons on its orbit.
Based on what they know, Mission Control estimates the pericynthion of the orbit will drop by 0.7 km to 16.1 km.
However, these predictions will turn out to be conservative.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: Okay; that looks pretty fair.
How about the - the out-of-plane or the cross track?
transcript.speaker: Scott

tokens: Dave is asking about another parameter of orbital mechanics.

If their orbit's inclination is not correct, then when it comes to the time of the descent to the surface, the spacecraft will aim to the north or south of the landing site rather than directly towards it.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: Stand by.
[Long pause.]
transcript.speaker: Henize

tokens: Dave, our out-of-plane data looks like about 2/10ths of a [nautical] mile [0.37 km] at PDI [Powered Descent Initiation] time.
transcript.speaker: Henize

tokens: Okay; that sounds pretty good.
Thank you.
[Long pause.]
transcript.speaker: Scott

tokens: Henize does not say whether this error is to the north or south but in 23 hours time, just before PDI, there will be a cross track error of 6.1 km (3.3 nautical miles) to the south.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: The Gamma-ray Spectrometer is due another ten-minute period when the Gain Step Shield is switched off so the operation of its discriminator can be calibrated.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: 15, we'd like High Gain [Antenna] to Auto, please.
transcript.speaker: Henize

tokens: Roger.
Hign Gain to Auto.
[Long pause.]
transcript.speaker: Scott

tokens: Dave, thinking about trajectories, you probably noticed the out-of-plane part of the DOI [burn].
They're telling me here that you burned a perfect LOI, but the state vector they gave you was slightly in error.
So, they got rid of - they - they made corrections for that during DOI so that we're standing pretty close to perfect now.
transcript.speaker: Henize

tokens: Okay.
I got to admit we did have some question about that [out-of-plane component] but figured you all had it in hand, as you usually do.
transcript.speaker: Scott

tokens: Roger.
Between a perfect crew in orbit up there and a perfect crew down here, we're doing pretty good so far.
Knock on wood.
transcript.speaker: Henize

tokens: Yeah.
We got a few miles to cover though.
transcript.speaker: Scott

tokens: Comm break.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: Houston, 15.
transcript.speaker: Irwin

tokens: Go ahead, 15.
transcript.speaker: Henize

tokens: Okay, Karl.
Guess I should bring you up to date on a couple of things here.
Everything went as planned on the - on the [far-side] terminator photos.
The Pan Camera and the Mapping Camera pass started on time and stopped on time.
Turn - Got the Mass Spec.
boom deployed and Gamma-ray boom deployed; the Mass Spec.
deployed in 2 minutes, 20 seconds; and the Gamma-ray deployed in 2 minutes, 28 seconds.
Got the Ion Source, On [means Standby], and the Logic Power, Off, at 85:05.
And that brings you up to date.
transcript.speaker: Worden

tokens: The Flight Plan included approximate expected times for the two booms to deploy and both of Al's reported times are about 20 seconds quicker than this.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: Beautiful, Al.
Sounds like all of that nice equipment is working real nice for us.
transcript.speaker: Henize

tokens: Sure does, Karl.
Be interested in what kind of data you're getting down there on it.
[Pause.]
transcript.speaker: Worden

tokens: OSO's [Orbital Science Officer's] two-word summary of it is that we're getting "beautiful data".
Incidentally, Al, if - if you'd like sometime, let us have, say - 12 to 24 hours operation, would you like to have a summary sometime tomorrow on some of the details?
transcript.speaker: Henize

tokens: Yes, indeed, Karl; sure would.
transcript.speaker: Worden

tokens: Okay; we'll get one together.
transcript.speaker: Henize

tokens: Yes, listen.
Skip it tomorrow; and, maybe day after tomorrow, we'll get a summary on that.
I'll be kind of interested in how it goes myself.
transcript.speaker: Worden

tokens: Very good.
transcript.speaker: Henize

tokens: Comm break.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: Houston, 15.
transcript.speaker: Worden

tokens: Go ahead, 15.
transcript.speaker: Henize

tokens: Roger, Karl.
For your information, you can see both of the - both of the booms at full extension out of window 5.
transcript.speaker: Worden

tokens: This is quite useful information as it will allow the crew to check by eye that the booms are extending properly throughout the orbital science period.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: About now, the Mass Spectrometer is powered up, though it will continue outgassing for another half hour.
The ion source, which is at the heart of the instrument's operation, has not yet been switched on.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: Hey, we're glad to hear that.
transcript.speaker: Henize

tokens: Yeah; they sure do look pretty sitting out there.
transcript.speaker: Worden

tokens: Roger.
transcript.speaker: Henize

tokens: Long comm break.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: 15, this is Houston.
We'd like to have you hold off on putting the Mass Spec.
Experiment switch to Standby, and we'd like to have the Discriminator, Low.
[Pause.]
transcript.speaker: Henize

tokens: According to the Flight Plan, the Mass Spectrometer Experiment switch was due to go to On at 85:25.
At 86:00, the Ion Source is due to be switched to On.
It is unclear why the Experiment switch is being put in Standby instead.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: Roger, Karl.
We'll hold off till you - cue us, and going Discriminator, Low, now.
transcript.speaker: Worden

tokens: Thank you, Al.
transcript.speaker: Henize

tokens: Comm break.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: 15, this is Houston.
If you'll give us Accept, we'll send up a new state vector.
[Pause.]
transcript.speaker: Henize

tokens: Roger, Houston.
You've got it.
transcript.speaker: Worden

tokens: And, - and if you've finished with dinner up there, and somebody can copy, I have a TEI-12 PAD.
transcript.speaker: Henize

tokens: Both of these requests, the uplink and the TEI-12 PAD, are as directed in the Flight Plan.
The latter is another abort PAD which, if required, would be used towards the end of the 12th revolution.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: Apollo 15 is nearing the Hadley landing site within the Apennine range.
These mountains are experiencing lunar dawn.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: Okay.
Stand by a minute, Karl.
[Long pause.]
transcript.speaker: Worden

tokens: Houston, we're making a low pass over the Apennines [mountains], and they're really something.
transcript.speaker: Scott

tokens: Roger.
[Pause.] Do they look like any terrestrial mountains you've ever seen?
[Pause.]
transcript.speaker: Henize

tokens: No.
[Pause.]
transcript.speaker: Scott

tokens: How about the slopes?
Are they generally steeper than you expect, or shallower than you expect on something like the Tetons?
[Pause.]
transcript.speaker: Henize

tokens: Say again, Karl.
I'm sorry; we were discussing the rille at that time - Hadley, that is.
transcript.speaker: Scott

tokens: Roger.
I - I was just trying to get a better feel for how the mountains look.
Are there - are they more - more craggy and rougher than something like the Tetons or do they give you some other appearance?
transcript.speaker: Henize

tokens: No.
As a matter of fact, from this altitude, even though we're low, they appear to be smooth and rounded.
There aren't any jagged peaks that we can see.
And even though they're cratered, and rough in texture on a small scale, they really don't look anything like the Alps or the San Juan's or any of the other familiar ranges we know.
transcript.speaker: Scott

tokens: Roger.
Is there anything that looks like bare rock on them.
[Pause.]
transcript.speaker: Henize

tokens: I think we can see some boulders, but there are no apparent jagged peaks that we can tell or that we can see from this - this particular altitude yet, although some of the - the shadows look fairly sharp.
transcript.speaker: Scott

tokens: Roger.
transcript.speaker: Henize

tokens: And, Karl, speaking of shadows, there seems to be enough light being reflected off the sides of the mountains around to supply some light down on the landing site.
And the rille is quite distinctive as we pass right over it.
transcript.speaker: Irwin

tokens: Beautiful.
That must be an eerie sight in a half-light.
transcript.speaker: Henize

tokens: The middle of the day on the Moon is searingly hot while the night is cold to great extremes.
Landing at Hadley is timed so that the Sun will have risen just 12°, giving oblique lighting with lots of shadows and aiding landmark identification during the final phase of the landing itself.
It also ensures that, as well as the Sun, the landscape isn't radiating huge amounts of heat to overload the EVA suit's cooling ability.
Though the Sun is illuminating the mountain peaks around the landing site, it has yet to reach the plain at Hadley.
The crew's ability to see detail in the shadows of the Moon is unmatched by the much narrower dynamic range of their cameras, and part of their brief is to describe that which cameras cannot record.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: The use of magazine R and its load of very fast black and white film at the last terminator crossing is an attempt to record these details on film.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: Well, it was, sort of.
transcript.speaker: Irwin

tokens: And, 15, you can have your computer back.
transcript.speaker: Henize

tokens: Roger.
transcript.speaker: Scott

tokens: Long comm break.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: 15, this is Houston.
[Long pause.]
transcript.speaker: Henize

tokens: 15, this is Houston.
How do you read.
transcript.speaker: Henize

tokens: Loud and clear, Houston.
transcript.speaker: Irwin

tokens: Roger.
We've finished picking up data on the Mass Spectrometer.
Leave the discriminator as it is, and we're ready to go to put the Experiment [switch] in Standby now.
[Pause.]
transcript.speaker: Henize

tokens: Understand; you're ready to put the Mass Spec.
in Standby.
transcript.speaker: Irwin

tokens: Roger.
And then you can start to put the blunt end forward, anytime you care to.
transcript.speaker: Henize

tokens: Roger; we're maneuvering now.
transcript.speaker: Irwin

tokens: Comm break.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: Since 84:00 GET, the spacecraft has been flying "sharp end forward" or plus-X forward attitude with the CM's apex pointing in the direction of flight.
Throughout the coming rest period it will be flying in the "blunt end forward" or minus-X forward attitude with the SPS engine facing the direction of flight.
This latter attitude is required for the proper operation of theMass Spectrometer.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: 15, this is Houston.
Dave, the Surgeon would like to have a special reading of the PRD [Personal Radiation Dosimeter] from you tonight.
Your reading last night was not in agreement with the other two crew members and they'd like to get another data point on you.
transcript.speaker: Henize

tokens: Okay.
We copy, Karl.
We'll give you all three readings.
transcript.speaker: Irwin

tokens: Roger.
They'll accept them gladly.
transcript.speaker: Henize

tokens: Comm break.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: 15, this is Houston.
Whenever you can copy, I have the TEI-12 PAD.
transcript.speaker: Henize

tokens: Stand by, Karl.
[Long pause.]
transcript.speaker: Irwin

tokens: Okay, Karl.
I'm ready to copy TEI-12.
transcript.speaker: Irwin

tokens: Roger.
TEI-12, SPS G&N; 38110; plus 0.58, plus 1.00; 101:36:08.38; plus 2845.0, minus 0380.2, minus 0063.8; 180, 107, 354; the rest is NA; 4-jet ullage for 12 seconds.
And this assumes the burn undocked and no circular burn, and the GET of landing is 196 hours at MPL.
transcript.speaker: Henize

tokens: An interpretation of the TEI-12 PAD follows:Purpose:This PAD specifies the information required for an early return to Earth, if necessary, towards the end of the twelfth lunar orbit.System:It would utilise the SPS engine under the control of the G&N system.CSM Weight (Noun 47):38,110 pounds (17,287 kg).Pitch and yaw trim (Noun 48):+0.58° and +1.00°.Time of ignition, TIG(Noun 33):101 hours, 36 minutes, 8.38 seconds.Change in velocity (Noun 81), fps (m/s):x, +2,845.0 (+867.2); y, -380.2 (-115.9); z, -63.8 (-19.4).
These velocity components are expressed with respect to thelocal vertical.Spacecraft attitude:Roll, 180°; Pitch, 107°; Yaw, 354°.
These attitude would be with respect to the orientation of the guidance platform.Henize notes that other items on the standard form are not applicable (NA) to this PAD.
Additional notes include the settlement of the SPS propellants be achieved by firing the plus-X thrusters on all four quads around the Service Module for 12 seconds.
The burn, if required, would be made after jettisoning the Lunar Module and with the spacecraft still in the descent orbit; i.e.
without a circularisation burn having been made.
Finally, Earth landing would then occur at about 196 hours into the mission and in the mid-Pacific landing area.
Note that this last item was not specified in the TEI-5 abort PAD, read up at081:34:19 GET.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: Okay.
TEI-12 readback: SPS/G&N 38110; plus 0.58, plus 1.00; 101:36:08.38; plus 2845.0, minus 0380.2, minus 0063.8; 180, 107, 354; 4 jets for 12 seconds.
Undocked, no circular burn.
Assumes landing at 196 GET at the MPL.
transcript.speaker: Irwin

tokens: That's all correct.
[Long pause.]
transcript.speaker: Henize

tokens: 15, we need the mode switch in Auto please.
[Long pause.]
transcript.speaker: Henize

tokens: Houston, 15.
What - what switch you talking about, Karl?
[Pause.]
transcript.speaker: Worden

tokens: That's the CMC Mode switch.
I guess we need to get into Auto before we can execute this maneuver.
transcript.speaker: Henize

tokens: That's affirmative.
And I'll go into Auto as soon as I complete the 40 degrees roll.
transcript.speaker: Scott

tokens: Roger.
transcript.speaker: Henize

tokens: Comm break.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: A fresh lithium hydroxide canister, number 9, is being placed in receptacle A while the exhausted one, number 7, is stowed in compartment B6.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: 15, Houston.
On the Mass Spectrometer operations down there at 59 minutes, we'd like to have you keep the Discriminator, Low, as it is now.
And, as the Ion Source goes On, we would like to have you pause in the Off position until you get our cue.
[Pause.]
transcript.speaker: Henize

tokens: Off is the central position of the Ion Source switch between Standby, its current position, and On.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: Mission Control are requesting that they keep the Discriminator for the Mass Spectrometer in its Low setting because of scattered sunlight causing excessive background counts in the instrument's detection electronics.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: Roger; understand.
You want the Discriminator left in Low, and want us to pause in Off on the Ion Source on your cue.
transcript.speaker: Worden

tokens: Roger.
And I - I - think you know that we'd probably - that we - we don't want to go into those operations until we have got into the correct attitude.
transcript.speaker: Henize

tokens: Comm break.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: 15, this is Houston.
transcript.speaker: Henize

tokens: Houston, 15; go ahead.
transcript.speaker: Worden

tokens: Couple of special notes on the hardware.

First of all, on - on the Systems Test Meter - we don't fully understand why that switch did funny things for us, and we have some back in - the fairly extensive tests going on down here still trying to understand it.
And we suggest that you leave the meter in the 5-B position until we do get some handle on what happened there.
transcript.speaker: Henize

tokens: Okay, Karl.
We copy that.
transcript.speaker: Worden

tokens: Even the smallest anomaly in one of the spacecraft's systems is exhaustively analysed to try and ensure there will be no surprises.

Both the SPAN (Spacecraft Analysis) room and the MER (Mission Evaluation Room) will be beavering away at diagrams and specifications of the Systems Test Meter switch, as well as working with the spacecraft contractors, trying to leave nothing to chance.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: Okay.
And I just heard the word that, if youreallyneed it, go ahead and use it, but if you don't have a strong reason for moving it, leave it as it is.
And on - We're - we're observing the radiator temperature - occasionally cycling above 80 degrees.
This is out of limits, and we don't really have a good solution for this at the present time.
We could think about turning on the evaporator, but that would foul up the Mass Spectrometer, and we'd rather not do that.

The only things that it's - that the high temperature would be bothering are the IMU PIPAs [Pulse Integrating Pendulous Accelerometers], and we have - we - Since the temperature is cycling up and down, cools off on the back side of the Moon, we - or on the dark side of the Moon, we - we think there's no - no imminent danger to that system.
So, our feeling on that is to leave the system as it is.
[Pause.]
transcript.speaker: Henize

tokens: As the Mass Spectrometer is intended to sample the very tenuous lunar atmosphere, it is highly susceptible to contamination from the spacecraft.
The two large radiators on the spacecraft are designed to lose only a certain amount of heat.

As the spacecraft cycles between lunar night and day, a supplemental cooling system mounted on the SM uses the evaporation of water in a vacuum to carry away excess heat, essentially dealing with the temperature peaks.
Although the Mass Spectrometer is carried on the end of a boom, it is feared the resulting water vapour cloud from the evaporator would saturate the experiment.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: Okay.
We understand.
transcript.speaker: Scott

tokens: Comm break.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: Flight Plan page 3-92.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: 86 hours into the mission is the official time for the crew to begin a 7.7 hour rest period.
It will be ten minutes before conversation with Karl Henize finishes and they will have a call seven and a quarter hours later.
The presleep checklist is being worked on just now which includes reading the status of batteries, RCS tanks and the PRDs.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: Houston, this is 15 with some onboard read-outs for you and PRD's.
transcript.speaker: Irwin

tokens: Go ahead, 15.
transcript.speaker: Henize

tokens: Roger.
Bat C is 37 [volts]; pyros A and B are both 37.5 [volts]; RCS 83, 82, 82 and 83 [per cent].
The PRD readings; 23072, 25014, and 08016.
[Long pause.]
transcript.speaker: Irwin

tokens: Roger, 15.
We copy all that.
[Long pause.]
transcript.speaker: Henize

tokens: Houston, this is 15.
The Ion Source is Off, and we're standing by for your word.
[Pause.]
transcript.speaker: Irwin

tokens: We copy, 15.
Stand by.
transcript.speaker: Henize

tokens: Comm break.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: 15, this is Houston.
Let's continue standing by on that Ion Source switch.
We'd like to get all the way into attitude before we go into the On position.
transcript.speaker: Henize

tokens: Roger.
transcript.speaker: Irwin

tokens: Long comm break.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: 15, this is Houston.
We're ready for the E-memory dump.
transcript.speaker: Henize

tokens: Okay, Karl.
Coming at you.
[Pause.]
transcript.speaker: Worden

tokens: The E-Memory dump is the final item on the presleep checklist.
It allows Mission Control to study the computer's memory and check its contents.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: And, 15, we're ready now for the Ion Source switch, On.
transcript.speaker: Henize

tokens: Ion Source going On.
transcript.speaker: Worden

tokens: With the Ion Source, On, the Mass Spectrometer begins full operation which will continue for the next nine hours.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: Designed to characterise the lunar atmosphere, the Mass Spectrometer measures the atomic weight of the atoms and molecules which enter an aperture on one side of the instrument.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: When the spacecraft is flown in an attitude that has the minus-X axis facing forward (i.e.
the SPS engine bell facing forward), particles from the lunar atmosphere are rammed into an inlet where they are electrically charged (ionised) by electrons from a filament source.
A magnet diverts the path of the resultant ion stream towards two detectors.
Simply stated, the heavier an atom or molecule, the more resistant its motion is to change by a magnetic field.
By measuring the deflection of the particle stream, the masses of its constituent parts can be determined.
The two detectors will detect particles in the mass range of 12 to 66 atomic mass units.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: The inlet is mounted on the opposite side of the instrument from the CSM to shield it from gases emanating from the spacecraft.
By changing the angle of attack of the inlet, it is possible to discriminate between CSM contaminants and genuine lunar atmosphere.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: According to the 'Apollo 15 Preliminary Science Report', little difference was detected between the inlet pointing into the flight path and pointing into the spacecraft's wake.
This implies that most of what the instrument detects is, essentially, pollution from the spacecraft.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: The later reference work, the 'Lunar Sourcebook' by Heiken, Vaniman and French, elaborates on this by pointing out how longer term surface experiments placed by Apollo 12, 14, 15 and 17 were deluged with contaminants from Apollo operations which made it very difficult to extract natural data from their results.

This is hardly surprising considering that estimates for the total mass of the natural lunar atmosphere are around 10 metric tonnes and a similar quantity of gases are released during each Apollo mission, mostly from operation of the descent and ascent engines.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: Houston, 15.
We're going to configure communications for sleep.
[Long pause.]
transcript.speaker: Irwin

tokens: 15, Houston.
Could you hold off on that until just after LOS.
transcript.speaker: Henize

tokens: Okay.
[Long pause.]
transcript.speaker: Irwin

tokens: Okay, 15.
Our last worry seems to be cleared up down here.
We've got nothing more to bother you with, and all we can do is wish you a good night's sleep.
transcript.speaker: Henize

tokens: Thank you, Karl.
Good night.
transcript.speaker: Irwin

tokens: Good night.
transcript.speaker: Henize

tokens: This is Apollo Control at 86 hours, 13 minutes.
We have lost the signal now on revolution 4.
We've said 'good night' to the crew.
Don't expect to talk to them until morning.
The voice carrier has been turned off.
We'll take this line down now at 86 hours, 14 minutes.
We're estimating the change of shift news conference for 11:15 pm Central Daylight Time in the News Center briefing room.
Flight Director Gene Kranz is preparing to relieve Milt Windler and his Flight Controller team.
At 86 hours, 14 minutes; this is Mission Control, Houston.
transcript.speaker: PAO

tokens: Apollo 15 will begin Rev 5 at about 086:36 during the next far-side pass.
The crew's rest period will continue until nearly the end of revolution 8.
transcript.speaker: Commentary
